title: Python Basics
description: Learn Python from scratch — variables, control flow, loops, and functions.

units.title: Python Foundations
units.description: Understand what Python is and write your very first programs.

lessons.title: What is Python?
lessons.description: Get to know Python — where it came from and how it works.

exercises.title: How Python runs
exercises.description: Unlike Java, Python doesn't compile to a separate executable — it runs line by line.
exercises.question: How does Python run your code?
options: It interprets code line by line at runtime
It compiles to bytecode first, then runs separately
It requires a class to run
exercises.explanation: Python is an interpreted language. The Python interpreter reads and executes your .py file directly.

exercises.title: Python is interpreted
exercises.description: Think about how Python executes your source code.
exercises.question: Python code is interpreted at runtime, not compiled to a separate executable first.
exercises.explanation: Python is interpreted — no separate compilation step. You run your .py file directly.

exercises.title: Python file extension
exercises.description: Python source files use a specific file extension.
exercises.question: What file extension do Python source files use?
exercises.explanation: .py is the extension for Python source files. For example: hello.py

exercises.title: Dynamic typing
exercises.description: Python handles types very differently from Java.
exercises.question: In Python, you must declare the type of every variable (e.g. int x = 5).
exercises.explanation: Python is dynamically typed. You just write x = 5 and Python figures out the type automatically.

exercises.title: Python file extension
exercises.description: Fill in the file extension for Python source files.
exercises.codeTemplate: # Save your Python program as: my_program.___
hints: Python files use a 2-letter extension that starts with 'p'.

lessons.title: Your First Program
lessons.description: Write and run your very first Python program.

exercises.title: print function
exercises.description: Python uses a built-in function to display output.
exercises.question: Which of these correctly prints 'Hello' in Python?
exercises.explanation: Python uses the built-in print() function. System.out.println is Java, console.log is JavaScript.

exercises.title: No main function required
exercises.description: Python programs work differently from Java.
exercises.question: Python requires a main() function to run a program.
exercises.explanation: Unlike Java, Python executes top-level code directly. No main() is required.

exercises.title: Complete the print
exercises.description: Fill in the function name used to display output in Python.
hints: This built-in function displays output to the screen.

exercises.title: Parentheses in print
exercises.description: print is a function — it requires parentheses around its argument.
hints: Functions in Python are called with parentheses: print('Hello!')

exercises.title: Hello, World!
exercises.description: Write a Python program that prints `Hello, World!` exactly.
exercises.starterCode: # Print Hello, World!
hints: Use the print() function.
print("Hello, World!")

lessons.title: Printing to the Screen
lessons.description: Learn how print() works and how to display multiple lines.

exercises.title: print function name
exercises.description: Fill in the name of the built-in function that displays output.
hints: The function that prints to the screen is built into Python.

exercises.title: Multiple print calls
exercises.description: Each call to print() outputs on its own line. What will this print?
hints: Each print() call adds a newline at the end automatically.

exercises.title: print adds a newline
exercises.description: Think about what print() does after displaying its content.
exercises.question: By default, print() in Python automatically moves to a new line after printing.
exercises.explanation: print() adds a '\n' at the end by default. You can change this with the end parameter.

exercises.title: print with end
exercises.description: Using `end=''` tells print() not to add a newline — both outputs join on one line.
hints: end='' removes the automatic newline, so both prints appear on the same line.

exercises.title: Print three lines
exercises.description:
Print these three words, each on its own line:
`Python`
`is`
`fun`
exercises.starterCode: # Print Python, is, fun on separate lines
hints: Call print() three times.
print('Python'), then print('is'), then print('fun')

lessons.title: Chapter 1 Review
lessons.description: Review everything from Chapter 1.

exercises.title: Correct print syntax
exercises.description: Review the correct way to print in Python.
exercises.question: Which of these correctly prints 'Hi' in Python?
exercises.explanation: Python uses print(). System.out.println is Java, echo is Bash, printf is C.

exercises.title: Python needs a class
exercises.description: Think about Python's structure compared to Java.
exercises.question: A Python program must be wrapped in a class to run.
exercises.explanation: Python doesn't require a class. Code at the top level runs directly.

exercises.title: Completing a print call
exercises.description: Fill in the function name to complete the statement.
hints: The built-in output function in Python.

exercises.title: Counting output
exercises.description: Trace through this program and predict what it prints.
hints: Each print() produces one line of output.

exercises.title: I love Python
exercises.description: Print exactly: `I love Python!`
exercises.starterCode: # Your code here
hints: print('I love Python!')

exercises.title: Two lines
exercises.description: Print `Hello` on the first line and `Python` on the second line.
exercises.starterCode: # Print Hello, then Python
hints: Use two separate print() calls.

units.title: Data and Variables
units.description: Store, retrieve, and compute with different types of data.

lessons.title: Basic Types
lessons.description: Python has four core built-in types: int, float, str, and bool.

exercises.title: Whole numbers
exercises.description: Python has different types for different kinds of data.
exercises.question: Which Python type stores whole numbers like 1, -5, or 100?
exercises.explanation: int stores whole numbers. float stores decimals like 3.14.

exercises.title: float and decimals
exercises.description: Know which Python type handles decimal numbers.
exercises.question: A float can store decimal numbers like 3.14 or 2.5.
exercises.explanation: float is Python's type for decimal (floating-point) numbers.

exercises.title: Text type
exercises.description: Python uses a specific type for text.
exercises.question: Which Python type stores text like 'Hello' or 'Python'?
exercises.explanation: str (short for string) is Python's type for text. Unlike Java, there is no separate char type.

exercises.title: Assignment operator
exercises.description: Fill in the operator that assigns a value to a variable.
hints: In Python, a single = sign assigns a value to a variable.

exercises.title: Printing a variable
exercises.description: Predict what this code prints.
hints: Printing a variable shows its value, not its name.

lessons.title: Declaring Variables
lessons.description: How to create, name, and assign variables in Python.

exercises.title: Variable name
exercises.description: Fill in the variable name that matches what is used in print.
hints: The variable name must match exactly what appears in print().

exercises.title: Variable output
exercises.description: When you print a variable, Python prints its value — not its name.

exercises.title: Boolean variable
exercises.description: Fill in the Python boolean value for 'true'.
hints: Python booleans are capitalized: True or False.

exercises.title: Reassigning variables
exercises.description: Once created, a variable's value can be changed.
exercises.question: You can change the value of a variable after it has been assigned.
exercises.explanation: Variables in Python can be reassigned at any time. Use ALL_CAPS by convention for constants.

exercises.title: Declare and print
exercises.description: Create a variable called `age` with value `20`, then print it.
exercises.starterCode: # Create age = 20 and print it
hints: Python syntax: name = value
age = 20, then print(age)

lessons.title: Arithmetic
lessons.description: Perform math operations with Python's arithmetic operators.

exercises.title: Addition
exercises.description: Predict the result of adding two variables.

exercises.title: Modulus operator
exercises.description: The % operator gives the remainder after division. Fill it in.
hints: 10 / 3 = 3 remainder 1. The operator that gives the remainder is %.

exercises.title: Integer division
exercises.description: Python uses // for integer (floor) division.
exercises.question: What is the result of `10 // 3` in Python?
exercises.explanation: // is Python's integer division operator. It drops the decimal: 10 // 3 = 3. Use / for 3.3333.

exercises.title: Modulus result
exercises.description: Predict the remainder from this division.
hints: 7 divided by 2 is 3 with remainder 1.

exercises.title: Sum and product
exercises.description: Given `a = 8` and `b = 5`, print their **sum** on one line, then their **product** on the next.
exercises.starterCode:
a = 8
b = 5
# Print sum, then product
exercises.solutionCode:
a = 8
b = 5
print(a + b)
print(a * b)
hints: Sum uses +, product uses *.
print(a + b), then print(a * b)

lessons.title: Chapter 2 Review
lessons.description: Review variables, types, and arithmetic.

exercises.title: Modulus result
exercises.description: Test your understanding of the % operator.
exercises.question: What is the result of `15 % 4` in Python?

exercises.title: Division in Python 3
exercises.description: Python 3 changed how the / operator works.
exercises.question: In Python 3, `7 / 2` returns `3.5` (not 3).
exercises.explanation: In Python 3, / always returns a float. Use // for integer division: 7 // 2 = 3.

exercises.title: Two variables
exercises.description: Fill in variable names that match what is used in print.
hints: The variable names must match exactly what appears in print(total - discount).

exercises.title: Reassignment
exercises.description: A variable's value can change. Trace through this code.
hints: x starts at 10. Then x = 10 + 5 = 15.

exercises.title: Subtract and multiply
exercises.description: Create `x = 6` and `y = 4`. Print `x - y`, then print `x * y`.
exercises.starterCode: # Create x and y, then print x-y and x*y
exercises.solutionCode:
x = 6
y = 4
print(x - y)
print(x * y)
hints: Create both variables, then use print() twice.

exercises.title: Float variable
exercises.description: Create a variable called `pi` with value `3.14`, then print it.
exercises.starterCode: # Create pi = 3.14 and print it
hints: pi = 3.14, then print(pi)

units.title: Text and Strings
units.description: Work with text using Python's str type.

lessons.title: String Basics
lessons.description: How to create and use string variables in Python.

exercises.title: Valid Python string
exercises.description: Python strings can use either single or double quotes.
exercises.question: Which of these is a valid Python string?
exercises.explanation: Python strings are wrapped in single or double quotes. 'Hello' and "Hello" are both valid.

exercises.title: Print a string variable
exercises.description: Fill in the function to display the greeting variable.
hints: The built-in function that displays output.

exercises.title: Single and double quotes
exercises.description: Python is flexible about which quotes you use.
exercises.question: In Python, both single quotes ('') and double quotes ("") can be used to create strings.
exercises.explanation: Python accepts both. 'hello' and "hello" are identical. Consistency is what matters.

exercises.title: Printing a string variable
exercises.description: When you print a string variable, Python shows the contents without quotes.
hints: print() shows the value, not the variable name or the quotes.

exercises.title: Declare and print a string
exercises.description: Create a variable called `city` with value `'Paris'` and print it.
exercises.starterCode: # Create city = 'Paris' and print it
hints: city = 'Paris', then print(city)

lessons.title: String Methods
lessons.description: Built-in methods and functions that work on strings.

exercises.title: len() function
exercises.description: Python has a built-in function to count characters in a string.
exercises.question: What does `len('Alice')` return?
exercises.explanation: 'Alice' has 5 characters: A-l-i-c-e. len() counts all of them.

exercises.title: upper() method
exercises.description: .upper() returns a new string with all letters capitalized.

exercises.title: Calling len()
exercises.description: Fill in the function that returns the number of characters.
hints: This 3-letter built-in function returns the length of a string.

exercises.title: lower() method
exercises.description: .lower() returns a new string with all letters lowercased.

exercises.title: Uppercase
exercises.description: Create `word = 'hello'` and print it in uppercase.
exercises.starterCode:
word = 'hello'
# Print word in uppercase
hints: Call .upper() on the string variable: word.upper()

lessons.title: Concatenation and f-strings
lessons.description: Join strings with + and format them with f-strings.

exercises.title: Joining strings
exercises.description: Python uses + to join strings together.
exercises.question: How do you join two strings in Python?
exercises.explanation: The + operator concatenates strings: 'Hello' + ' World' = 'Hello World'.

exercises.title: Concatenation result
exercises.description: Two strings joined with + are merged into one.

exercises.title: f-string prefix
exercises.description: Fill in the single-character prefix that makes this an f-string.
hints: f-strings start with the letter f before the opening quote.

exercises.title: f-string output
exercises.description: f-strings embed variables directly inside curly braces {}.
hints: {name} is replaced with the value of the name variable.

exercises.title: Full name with f-string
exercises.description: Given `first = 'Python'` and `last = 'Learner'`, print them as `Python Learner` using an f-string.
exercises.starterCode:
first = 'Python'
last = 'Learner'
# Print full name using an f-string
hints: Use an f-string: f'{first} {last}'
print(f'{first} {last}')

lessons.title: Chapter 3 Review
lessons.description: Review strings, string methods, and f-strings.

exercises.title: String indexing
exercises.description: Python strings are indexed starting from 0.
exercises.question: What does `'Hello'[0]` return in Python?
exercises.explanation: Index 0 is the first character. 'Hello'[0] = 'H'.

exercises.title: String comparison with ==
exercises.description: Unlike Java, Python's == works correctly for string comparison.
exercises.question: In Python, you can use `==` to compare the content of two strings.
exercises.explanation: Python's == correctly compares string content. Unlike Java, there is no need for .equals().

exercises.title: String length
exercises.description: Fill in the function that returns the number of characters.
hints: The function that counts characters is built into Python.

exercises.title: f-string review
exercises.description: Predict the output of this f-string.
hints: {s} is replaced with the value of s.

exercises.title: Greeting
exercises.description: Create `greeting = 'Hello'` and `target = 'Python'`. Print them joined with a space and exclamation mark using an f-string.
exercises.starterCode:
greeting = 'Hello'
target = 'Python'
# Print using an f-string
hints: Use: print(f'{greeting} {target}!')

exercises.title: String length
exercises.description: Create `text = 'programming'` and print its length.
exercises.starterCode:
text = 'programming'
# Print the length of text
hints: Use len(text) to get the character count.

units.title: Making Decisions
units.description: Control what your program does using booleans and if/else.

lessons.title: Booleans and Comparison
lessons.description: The bool type holds True or False — the foundation of all decisions.

exercises.title: Boolean values
exercises.description: Python's bool type has exactly two valid values.
exercises.question: Which of these is a valid Python boolean literal?
exercises.explanation: Python booleans are capitalized: True and False. Lowercase 'true' would be treated as a variable name.

exercises.title: Boolean range
exercises.description: Python's bool type has only two possible values.
exercises.question: A Python bool variable can only hold two values: True or False.
exercises.explanation: bool is Python's logical type — exactly two states: True and False.

exercises.title: Declare a bool
exercises.description: Fill in the Python boolean value meaning 'it is not raining'.
hints: Python booleans are capitalized. Not raining = False.

exercises.title: Comparison result
exercises.description: Comparison operators like > produce a bool result.
hints: 5 > 3 is True, so x is True.

exercises.title: Equality check
exercises.description: The == operator checks equality and returns a bool.
hints: 10 is not equal to 20, so the result is False.

lessons.title: if Statements
lessons.description: Run code only when a condition is true.

exercises.title: if keyword
exercises.description: Every conditional statement starts with a specific keyword.
exercises.question: What keyword starts a conditional statement in Python?
exercises.explanation: `if` is the keyword. The condition is followed by a colon: if condition:

exercises.title: if colon
exercises.description: Python if statements end the condition line with a colon.
hints: Python uses a colon after the condition, then indented code below.

exercises.title: if/else branch
exercises.description: Only one branch executes — the one whose condition is true.
hints: 3 > 5 is False, so the else branch runs.

exercises.title: Age check
exercises.description: Which branch will run for age = 20?
hints: 20 >= 18 is True.

exercises.title: Pass or Fail
exercises.description: Given `score = 75`: if score >= 60, print `Pass`. Otherwise print `Fail`.
exercises.starterCode:
score = 75
# if score >= 60, print Pass, else print Fail
hints: Use if score >= 60:
Remember to indent the print() calls with 4 spaces.

lessons.title: if-else and elif
lessons.description: Handle multiple conditions with elif.

exercises.title: elif keyword
exercises.description: Python uses a single keyword for 'else if'.
exercises.question: Which keyword adds an additional condition after `if` in Python?
exercises.explanation: Python uses `elif` (a contraction of 'else if') — one word, unlike Java's two-word 'else if'.

exercises.title: Three-way branch
exercises.description: Trace through the chain: which condition is true first?
hints: 5 > 10 is False. 5 > 3 is True — so 'medium' prints.

exercises.title: Grade chain
exercises.description: Fill in Python's keyword that links the second condition.
hints: Python's 'else if' is written as a single 4-letter word.

exercises.title: Sign check
exercises.description: What will print for n = 0?
hints: 0 is not > 0 and not < 0, so the else block runs.

exercises.title: Temperature check
exercises.description:
Given `temp = 30`:
- If temp > 35, print `Hot`
- elif temp > 20, print `Warm`
- Otherwise print `Cool`
exercises.starterCode:
temp = 30
# Write if/elif/else here
hints: Use if / elif / else structure.
30 > 35 is False. 30 > 20 is True — so 'Warm' should print.

lessons.title: Chapter 4 Review
lessons.description: Review booleans, if/else, and elif chains.

exercises.title: Equality operator
exercises.description: Don't confuse assignment with equality comparison.
exercises.question: Which operator checks if two values are equal in Python?
exercises.explanation: = is assignment. == is equality comparison. `is` checks object identity, which is different.

exercises.title: else always runs
exercises.description: Think carefully about when the else block executes.
exercises.question: The else block always runs when an if statement is present.
exercises.explanation: The else block only runs when all preceding if/elif conditions are False.

exercises.title: Greater-or-equal
exercises.description: Fill in the comparison operator that means 'greater than or equal to'.
hints: The operator for 'greater than or equal' uses two characters.

exercises.title: Not-equal check
exercises.description: `!=` means 'not equal'. What will print?
hints: 10 != 5 is True.

exercises.title: Even or odd
exercises.description: Given `x = 8`: print `even` if `x % 2 == 0`, otherwise print `odd`.
exercises.starterCode:
x = 8
# Check if x is even or odd
hints: Use x % 2 == 0 to check if a number is even.

exercises.title: Positive, negative, zero
exercises.description: Given `n = -5`: print `positive` if n > 0, `negative` if n < 0, or `zero` if n == 0.
exercises.starterCode:
n = -5
# Use if/elif/else
hints: Use if / elif / else with three conditions.

units.title: Loops
units.description: Repeat actions automatically with for and while loops.

lessons.title: for Loops
lessons.description: Use for loops with range() to repeat code a fixed number of times.

exercises.title: range() function
exercises.description: Python's range() generates a sequence of numbers for loops.
exercises.question: What does `range(5)` produce?
exercises.explanation: range(5) produces 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — five numbers starting from 0.

exercises.title: for loop output
exercises.description: A for loop using range(1, 4). What prints?
hints: range(1, 4) generates 1, 2, 3 — it stops before 4.

exercises.title: range argument
exercises.description: Fill in the range argument so 'Hello' prints exactly 3 times.
hints: range(n) runs the loop n times starting from 0.

exercises.title: range with start and stop
exercises.description: range(start, stop) generates from start up to (but not including) stop.
hints: range(3, 6) gives 3, 4, 5 — stops before 6.

exercises.title: Count to 5
exercises.description: Use a for loop to print numbers 1 through 5, one per line.
exercises.starterCode: # Use a for loop to print 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
hints: Use range(1, 6) to get numbers 1 through 5.
for i in range(1, 6):  print(i)

lessons.title: while Loops
lessons.description: Repeat code while a condition stays true.

exercises.title: while loop purpose
exercises.description: A while loop keeps running its body as long as the condition is true.
exercises.question: What does a while loop do?
options: Repeats code while a condition is True
Runs code once if the condition is True
exercises.explanation: while checks the condition before each iteration. If it's False from the start, the body never runs.

exercises.title: Counting while loop
exercises.description: Trace through this loop step by step.
hints: i starts at 0, prints 0, 1, 2. When i = 3 the condition is False.

exercises.title: while keyword
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that starts a condition-controlled loop.
hints: The keyword is the same as the English word meaning 'as long as'.

exercises.title: Infinite loop
exercises.description: A loop whose condition never becomes False runs forever.
exercises.question: `while True:` creates an infinite loop.
exercises.explanation: True is always True, so the loop never stops unless you use break.

exercises.title: While count
exercises.description: Use a while loop to print numbers 1 through 5, one per line.
exercises.starterCode:
i = 1
# while i <= 5, print i and increment
hints: Start i at 1, loop while i <= 5.
Don't forget to increment: i += 1 inside the loop.

lessons.title: Loop Patterns
lessons.description: Use break, continue, and loops to solve common patterns.

exercises.title: break statement
exercises.description: `break` gives you a way to exit a loop early.
exercises.question: What does `break` do inside a loop?
exercises.explanation: break exits the loop entirely. Execution continues after the loop body.

exercises.title: break in action
exercises.description: The loop stops as soon as `break` is reached.
hints: When i == 3, break runs before print, so 3 is never printed.

exercises.title: continue in action
exercises.description: `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next.
hints: When i == 2, continue skips print — so 2 is never printed.

exercises.title: Print squares
exercises.description: Fill in the operator to print the square of each number.
exercises.codeTemplate:
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i ___ i)
hints: Multiplying a number by itself gives you its square.

exercises.title: Sum 1 to 10
exercises.description: Use a for loop to compute the sum of numbers 1 through 10. Print only the final sum.
exercises.starterCode:
total = 0
# Add i to total for i in range(1, 11)

print(total)
exercises.solutionCode:
total = 0
for i in range(1, 11):
    total += i
print(total)
hints: Use total += i inside the loop to accumulate.

lessons.title: Chapter 5 Review
lessons.description: Review for loops, while loops, and loop patterns.

exercises.title: Loop choice
exercises.description: Picking the right loop for the job makes code cleaner.
exercises.question: Which loop is best when you know the exact number of iterations?
exercises.explanation: for loops with range() are designed for a known count. while loops suit unknown repetitions.

exercises.title: continue behavior
exercises.description: Understand what continue does vs break.
exercises.question: `continue` skips the current iteration and moves to the next one.
exercises.explanation: continue skips only the current pass. break would exit the loop entirely.

exercises.title: range with step
exercises.description: Fill in the step to make range generate even numbers 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.
hints: range(start, stop, step) — a step of 2 skips every other number.

exercises.title: Accumulator
exercises.description: Trace through this accumulator loop.
exercises.code:
total = 0
for i in range(1, 5):
    total += i
print(total)

exercises.title: Countdown
exercises.description: Use a for loop to print a countdown from 5 down to 1.
hints: Use range(5, 0, -1) to count down.
range(start, stop, step) with step = -1

exercises.title: Powers of 2
exercises.description: Use a while loop to print: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 (each on its own line).
exercises.starterCode:
x = 1
# Print x while x <= 16, then double x
hints: Double x each iteration: x *= 2
while x <= 16:  print(x);  x *= 2

units.title: Functions
units.description: Write reusable blocks of code with functions.

lessons.title: Function Basics
lessons.description: Functions let you name and reuse blocks of code.

exercises.title: def keyword
exercises.description: Python uses a specific keyword to define functions.
exercises.question: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?
exercises.explanation: def is the keyword for defining functions in Python: def my_function():

exercises.title: def keyword is required
exercises.description: You use a specific keyword before the function name.
exercises.question: In Python, you use `def` to define a function.
exercises.explanation: def is short for 'define'. It tells Python you're creating a new function.

exercises.title: Function definition
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that defines a function.
hints: 3-letter keyword that starts every function definition in Python.

exercises.title: Calling a function
exercises.description: The function body runs each time you call it.
hints: say_hi() is called twice, so 'Hi!' prints twice.

exercises.title: Define and call
exercises.description: Define a function called `greet` that prints `Hello from a function!`. Then call it.
exercises.starterCode:
# Define greet() here

# Call greet()
hints: def greet():  (don't forget the colon)
Call it with: greet()

lessons.title: Parameters
lessons.description: Pass data into functions using parameters.

exercises.title: What are parameters
exercises.description: Parameters let you customize a function's behavior each call.
exercises.question: What are parameters in Python?
exercises.explanation: Parameters are inputs declared in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values passed when calling.

exercises.title: String parameter
exercises.description: The parameter `name` holds whatever value is passed in.
hints: name receives 'Alice' as its value.

exercises.title: Using parameters
exercises.description: Fill in the parameter names used in the function body.
hints: Use the parameter names declared in the function signature.

exercises.title: int parameter
exercises.description: The function receives n and computes its square.

exercises.title: double_it function
exercises.description: Define a function `double_it(n)` that prints `n * 2`. Call it with argument `7`.
exercises.starterCode:
# Define double_it(n) here

# Call double_it(7)
hints: def double_it(n):  print(n * 2)
Call it with: double_it(7)

lessons.title: Return Values
lessons.description: Get data back from a function using return.

exercises.title: return keyword
exercises.description: Functions can send a value back to the caller.
exercises.question: Which keyword sends a value back from a Python function?
exercises.explanation: `return value` exits the function and sends the value back to the caller.

exercises.title: return statement
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that sends the result back to the caller.
hints: 6-letter keyword that exits the function with a value.

exercises.title: Using a return value
exercises.description: The return value is stored in a variable and then printed.
exercises.code:
def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

result = multiply(3, 4)
print(result)

exercises.title: Functions without return
exercises.description: In Python, every function returns something.
exercises.question: A Python function with no return statement returns None.
exercises.explanation: If a function doesn't explicitly return a value, Python implicitly returns None.

exercises.title: get_max function
exercises.description: Complete `get_max(a, b)` so it returns the larger value. The call is already written.
exercises.starterCode:
def get_max(a, b):
    # return the larger value
    pass

print(get_max(6, 9))
hints: Use if a > b: return a  then return b
9 > 6, so get_max(6, 9) should return 9.

lessons.title: Chapter 6 Review
lessons.description: Review functions, parameters, and return values.

exercises.title: Default parameters
exercises.description: Python lets you give parameters a default value.
exercises.question: What does `def greet(name='World'):` mean?
options: name defaults to 'World' if not provided
exercises.explanation: Default parameters let you call greet() without arguments — name will be 'World' automatically.

exercises.title: Functions reduce repetition
exercises.description: One of the biggest benefits of functions.
exercises.question: Functions help avoid writing the same code in multiple places.
exercises.explanation: That's the DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself. Write it once, call it many times.

exercises.title: Function with return
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword that sends back the greeting string.
hints: The keyword that exits a function with a value.

exercises.title: Cube function
exercises.description: Trace through the function call.

exercises.title: is_even function
exercises.description: Complete `is_even(n)` so it returns `True` if n is even. The calls are already written.
exercises.starterCode:
def is_even(n):
    # return True if n % 2 == 0
    pass

print(is_even(4))
print(is_even(7))
exercises.solutionCode:
def is_even(n):
    return n % 2 == 0

print(is_even(4))
print(is_even(7))
hints: A number is even if n % 2 == 0.
return n % 2 == 0  — this evaluates to True or False.

exercises.title: greet_user function
exercises.description: Complete `greet_user(name)` so it prints `Welcome, Alex!`. The call is already written.
exercises.starterCode:
def greet_user(name):
    # print: Welcome, [name]!
    pass

greet_user('Alex')
hints: Use an f-string: f'Welcome, {name}!'
print(f'Welcome, {name}!')
